driver.body:
The first driver is the least glamorous and the most decisive: there is exactly **one data platform, not six** [S-081]. This is not a preference for tidiness; it is the load-bearing precondition for everything the Data & AI Platform claims to do, because the moat identity M = D × B × L × T multiplies its terms rather than adding them, and a term cannot be multiplied by a domain that cannot read it.

The conglomerate failure mode is not strategic disagreement — it is six teams each standing up their own warehouse, their own feature definitions, their own model registry, so that "rider dwell time" means one thing in D1 and a subtly different thing in D3, and the join that would have produced a feature never happens [S-081]. Divergence of that kind is silent: nobody decides to fragment the data, it fragments by default the moment six schemas are allowed to drift. Domain 6's architecture exists to make that divergence structurally impossible — one ingestion spine, one feature store, one serving layer, built so the pipes come before anything that flows through them.

mechanisms.body:
Every physical-world interaction across the estate emits an event: a ride start, a charge session, a courier accept, a card authorisation, a device heartbeat [S-082]. All six domains publish to a **single append-only event bus** — Kepler calls it the Interaction Log — under one canonical schema: entity_id, geohash, t, domain, event_type, payload, trust_context [S-601]. The schema is the whole trick. Because every domain speaks the same seven-field grammar, an event from D2 charging and an event from D4 payments land in the same log, keyed the same way, and become joinable without a translation layer that would never have been built. At 28.4m MAU across 42 cities, the spine ingests ~3.9bn events/day at peak [S-082] — a volume that only stays coherent because there is one grammar rather than six.

The point of one grammar is the **point-in-time join**: any feature can be reconstructed as it was known at decision time, so a fraud model never trains on settlement data that only existed after the authorisation [S-602]. That guardrail against label leakage is not a modelling nicety — it is what makes cross-domain signal safe to use at all. It is what lets D4 signals safely sharpen D1–D3 without leakage, a cross-feed that is impossible in a six-warehouse world because the timestamps never reconcile [S-602]. The architecture diagram is the physical shape of the platform, and every later mechanism is a path through it [S-601].

The Interaction Log is raw; the feature store is where **breadth B actually lives** [S-083]. Raw events are not features — breadth is not the count of streams, it is the count of computed, reusable signals every domain can draw on. The feature store holds two mirrored surfaces: an offline store (columnar, for training) and an online store (key-value, sub-10ms, for serving), guaranteeing train/serve parity so a model sees the same value in production that it saw in training [S-603]. A feature computed once — say geohash_demand_z_15m, the 15-minute demand z-score for a location — is available to D1 routing, D2 siting, and D3 matching from the same row [S-603].

This is the precise mechanism by which B compounds instead of fragmenting: each new domain does not add a warehouse, it adds columns other domains can read [S-083]. In a six-warehouse world that same z-score is computed three times, three subtly different ways, and shared by none; here it is one row read by three consumers, and the marginal domain lifts the breadth term for all of them.

Note feat_entity.trust_score and feat_entity.pay_default_pd: those columns are written by D4 and read by D1 — to decide which riders can go cash-free — the payments-closes-the-loop coupling made concrete as two columns in one table [S-083]. The cross-feed the flywheel depends on is not an integration project; it is a column reference.

Breadth is inert until it is served back — a feature store no domain can query at decision time is just an expensive archive. The serving layer exposes every model behind **one low-latency API with a shared contract**, so a domain requests a decision — route, site, match, authorise, flag — and receives it in <60ms p95 without knowing which features or which upstream domains produced it [S-604]. The abstraction is the point: a consumer domain does not integrate against five other domains, it integrates against one contract, and the hub resolves the rest. This is the architectural expression of "all five draw from the hub" [S-604].

It also means a model improvement — better ETA residuals from richer D5 telemetry, say — propagates to every consumer at once, rather than being re-implemented five times [S-604]. One improvement, served through one API, lifts every domain that reads it simultaneously; that is compounding rather than copying. Driver 1 has now built the pipes — one spine, one feature store, one serving layer; Driver 2 explains what flows through them and why it compounds [S-084].
